dataset: Contested Liberalisms in Mexico — structured analytical tables
author: Luis Bourguet
description: Machine-readable versions of the five analytical tables from the article. Coupling codes in table3: decoupled | in_dispute | synthesis (color legend red/orange/blue respectively).

table1a_clv_general_instrument.title: Table 1a. Pegoraro & Rinella's sources of constitutional legitimation (general instrument)

rows.basis_of_the_bond: Obligation from historical continuity; the past validates the present
rows.cpeum_anchor: Art. 39 (sovereignty in the people, original DOF 05-02-1917); 1917 Constituent Congress Preamble

rows.maxim: Behave according to the pacts (pacta sunt servanda)
rows.basis_of_the_bond: Norm binds because recipients consented; autonomy of the will
rows.cpeum_anchor: Art. 40 (representative, democratic, federal Republic); Art. 135 (supermajority amendment rule)

rows.basis_of_the_bond: Transcendent authority; the sacred sanctions the norm
rows.cpeum_anchor: Arts. 24 and 130 (freedom of religion; strict Church/State separation — the Reforma's constitutional legacy)

rows.basis_of_the_bond: Authority of the sage, judge, or expert; rational, law-bound argumentation — primary source in a civil-law order
rows.cpeum_anchor: Arts. 94–107 (judiciary; jurisprudencia); Art. 28 (autonomous bodies, partially extinguished DOF 20 Dec 2024); Art. 1 (human rights via courts)

rows.basis_of_the_bond: The apex translates political values into norms; citizens obey as the group's voice (Ehrlich: 'conscious of serving the ruler, and only sometimes of serving the group')

table1b_clv_applied.title: Table 1b. The CLV applied to the two Mexican liberalisms

rows.popular_liberalism_4T: Juarista/Reforma lineage claimed as popular-liberal inheritance; the 4T as its authentic continuation
rows.procedural_liberalism: Liberal-constitutional tradition read as the rule-of-law and separation-of-powers heritage
rows.tension_what_is_at_stake: Both claim the liberal tradition; neither can accuse the other of abandoning it. Art. 39 sustains both readings

rows.popular_liberalism_4T: Consent reactivated as popular sovereignty and plebiscitary mandate (Art. 39); electoral pact as mandate for transformation
rows.procedural_liberalism: Constitutional compact (Art. 40, Art. 135) as the binding agreement; procedure as the locus of consent
rows.tension_what_is_at_stake: Both invoke the Agreement of the pact with incompatible readings: mandate-to-transform vs. compact-to-constrain

rows.popular_liberalism_4T: Largely absent at doctrinal level; moral-traditionalist register (Cartilla Moral) borrows its tone
rows.procedural_liberalism: Rawlsian neutrality: law separated from morality
rows.tension_what_is_at_stake: Not a primary axis of conflict; noted where moral-traditionalist tone appears

rows.popular_liberalism_4T: Suppressed: technical/law-bound authority (SCJN, INE, autonomous bodies) recoded as elitist obstacle to the popular will
rows.procedural_liberalism: Dominant: judicial independence, jurisprudencia, autonomous expert oversight as reason institutionalised
rows.tension_what_is_at_stake: The reason component is what the 4T erodes and procedural liberalism defends. Primary site of the conflict

rows.popular_liberalism_4T: Dominant source: presidency as authentic voice of the people sutures the Ehrlichian fracture; mañaneras as daily re-enactment
rows.procedural_liberalism: Formally rejected (rule of law, not of persons); in practice a softer technocratic-leadership version
rows.tension_what_is_at_stake: The Ehrlichian fracture the 4T rhetorically dissolves and procedural liberalism insists must remain visible. Art. 39 sustains both

rows.popular_liberalism_4T: N/A
rows.procedural_liberalism: N/A
rows.tension_what_is_at_stake: Omission is principled: Glenn (2014) confirms Mexico's civil-law tradition. The 2024 judicial reform moved the system further from any common-law analogy

rows.popular_liberalism_4T: Dominant: Tradition + Agreement (electoral pact) + Authority. Reason suppressed. High mobilising capacity; low counter-majoritarian resilience
rows.procedural_liberalism: Dominant: Reason + Agreement (constitutional compact). Authority formally rejected. High procedural stability; low popular mobilising energy
rows.tension_what_is_at_stake: Constitutionally undecidable on textual grounds alone: the text contains both profiles. Resolution requires winning the political-legitimacy argument — Art. 135 is the only operative procedural arbiter

table2_alterity_todorov.title: Table 2. Two liberalisms and their construction of the other (after Todorov 1982)

rows.popular_liberalism_4T: The other (the 'oligarchy' and its instruments) is judged morally bad, not merely different
rows.procedural_liberalism: The other (the popular majority) is judged not bad but epistemically deficient, to be guided

rows.popular_liberalism_4T: Assimilation: the people folded into one moral-national subject; dissenters redescribed as outside the nation
rows.procedural_liberalism: Containment: the majority bounded by counter-majoritarian institutions and procedure

rows.popular_liberalism_4T: Claims intimate knowledge of 'the people' while refusing to know its internal plurality
rows.procedural_liberalism: Characteristic failure: refuses to know the popular subject on its own terms

rows.popular_liberalism_4T: Moral-national belonging: those faithful to the popular project
rows.procedural_liberalism: Rights-bearing citizenship: equal individuals under law

rows.popular_liberalism_4T: Moral exclusion: dissent recoded as betrayal / antinational
rows.procedural_liberalism: Procedural exclusion: the majority distrusted, distanced from direct rule

table3_community_layers_coupling.title: Table 3. Community layers, constitutional alignment and structural decoupling (after Anderson 1983 & Pegoraro/Rinella 2016)

rows.popular_liberalism_4T: Moral-historical 'we'; Juarista lineage; fidelity, not status
rows.procedural_liberalism: Abstract citizens; no thick identity claim

rows.popular_liberalism_4T: 4T as fourth act of national epic; mañaneras as ritual
rows.procedural_liberalism: No equivalent narrative; procedure, not story

rows.popular_liberalism_4T: Cartilla Moral; 'no mentir, no robar'; thick ethics
rows.procedural_liberalism: Rawlsian neutrality: law separated from morality

rows.central_question: Who recognises the same constitutional frame?
rows.popular_liberalism_4T: Claims CPEUM; rewrites it via Art. 135
rows.procedural_liberalism: CPEUM as binding compact; institutions as guarantors
rows.coupling_status: In dispute — both claim the text

rows.popular_liberalism_4T: SCJN, INE recoded as elite; expertise dismantled
rows.procedural_liberalism: Autonomous bodies as reason institutionalised
rows.coupling_status: Decoupled — 4T dismantles; procedural depends on it

rows.popular_liberalism_4T: Uses frame to remake it; Art. 135 as instrument
rows.procedural_liberalism: Defends existing constitutional architecture

rows.popular_liberalism_4T: Each reform existential: transformation or neoporfirismo
rows.procedural_liberalism: No eschatology; procedural project lacks urgency

rows.central_question: Who shares a definition of social reality?
rows.popular_liberalism_4T: Redefines people, nation, democracy
rows.procedural_liberalism: Accepts existing definitions; no ontological contest

rows.popular_liberalism_4T: Aligns: belonging, imagined, moral, destiny, ontological. Gap: juridical, epistemic. High mobilising power; low institutional stability
rows.procedural_liberalism: Aligns: juridical, epistemic, constitutional. Gap: belonging, imagined, moral, destiny. High procedural stability; low popular rootedness
rows.coupling_status: STRUCTURAL DECOUPLING: non-overlapping layers. Resolution: bridge both sets or hegemonic victory. Art. 135 arbitrates; cannot reunite a split 'we'

table4_reform_chronology.title: Table 4. Constitutional reforms of 'Plan C', 2024–2025 (all enacted via Art. 135)

rows.articles_and_content: 19 articles (17, 94–101, 105, 107…); popular election of ministers, magistrates and judges; SCJN reduced 11→9; Tribunal of Judicial Discipline created
12 articles (13, 16, 21, 32, 55, 73, 76, 78, 82, 89, 123, 129); Guard incorporated into SEDENA; civilian character removed; overrides prior SCJN ruling
Art. 28 et al.; extinguishes 7 autonomous bodies (INAI, COFECE, IFT, CRE, CNH, CONEVAL, MEJOREDU); functions to the Executive; expressly invokes Art. 135
Art. 40; new paragraphs against foreign intervention in elections and external investigation
